root_text.ud7.4:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā sāvatthiyaṁ viharati jetavane anāthapiṇḍikassa ārāme.
root_text.ud7.4:1.3: Tena kho pana samayena sāvatthiyā manussā yebhuyyena kāmesu sattā (…) rattā giddhā gathitā mucchitā ajjhosannā andhīkatā sammattakajātā kāmesu viharanti.
root_text.ud7.4:2.1: Atha kho bhagavā pubbaṇhasamayaṁ nivāsetvā pattacīvaramādāya sāvatthiṁ piṇḍāya pāvisi.
root_text.ud7.4:2.2: Addasā kho bhagavā sāvatthiyā te manusse yebhuyyena kāmesu satte ratte giddhe gathite mucchite ajjhopanne andhīkate sammattakajāte kāmesu viharante.
root_text.ud7.4:3.1: Atha kho bhagavā etamatthaṁ viditvā tāyaṁ velāyaṁ imaṁ udānaṁ udānesi:

translation_text.ud7.4:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery.
translation_text.ud7.4:1.3: Now at that time most of the people in Sāvatthī clung to sensual pleasures. Lustful, greedy, tied, infatuated, attached, and blinded, they lived completely addicted to sensual pleasures.
translation_text.ud7.4:2.1: Then the Buddha robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, entered Sāvatthī for alms.
translation_text.ud7.4:2.2: He saw how attached the humans were.
translation_text.ud7.4:3.1: Then, understanding this matter, on that occasion the Buddha expressed this heartfelt sentiment:
translation_text.ud7.4:4.1: “Blinded by sensual pleasures, wrapped in a net,
translation_text.ud7.4:4.2: they are smothered over by craving;
translation_text.ud7.4:4.3: bound by the kinsman of the negligent,
translation_text.ud7.4:4.4: like a fish caught in a funnel-net trap.
translation_text.ud7.4:4.5: They chase old age and death,
translation_text.ud7.4:4.6: like a suckling calf its mother.”
